questions.prompt: How is this training organised?
choices: Short lessons grouped into topics, with a checkpoint between topics and a single final scenario assessment at the end.

choices.label: Three courses (l1, l2, l3) with weekly cohorts.
A single long course you work through linearly without breaks.
choices.rationale: Split into topics to support sittings.

choices.label: Two tracks (EDR and ITDR) you pick one of.

questions.explanation: Topics of short lessons with checkpoints and a final scenario assessment.

questions.prompt: "The final assessment IS the sign-off to work live tickets." Why is that wrong?
choices: The final is a recommendation of readiness, not a sign-off. Your MSP has its own shadow-and-review process.

choices.label: Because the assessment is too easy to count as a sign-off.
choices.rationale: The issue is what kind of input the score is.

choices.label: Because Huntress runs the assessment and your MSP isn't involved.
choices.rationale: The training assessment is the training's, not the vendor's.

choices.label: Because the score isn't recorded anywhere.
choices.rationale: It is recorded; that's how the LMS gates.

questions.explanation: LMS recommendation vs. MSP sign-off. The two layers don't substitute for each other.

questions.prompt: Which field of a questions-list entry pulls the most weight, and why?
choices: "What you tried." It blocks trivial questions, gives the senior usable context, and builds the habit of trying before asking.

choices.label: "Where." Without the lesson number the senior can't look anything up.
"What you didn't understand."
choices.rationale: Necessary, but "what you tried" pulls the most weight.

choices.label: The timestamp.

questions.explanation: Three benefits in one line.

questions.prompt: Roughly what percentage of daily Huntress ticket volume does the helpdesk tech handle independently after this course?

choices.label: About 50%.

choices: About 95% — the remaining 5% (Critical investigation, detection tuning, commercial decisions, irreversible policy changes) escalates.

choices.label: About 25%.

questions.explanation: 95% in scope; the 5% above the ceiling stays escalated.

questions.prompt: The two-question test that holds the escalation ceiling together asks what?
choices: Is this reversible, and do I understand why it's the right action? If either answer is no, escalate.

choices.label: Is this Critical, and has the SOC closed the incident?
Will the customer notice, and will my senior approve?
Did the SOC sign the report, and is my MSP licensed for the action?

questions.explanation: Reversibility plus understanding. If either answer is no, escalate.

questions.prompt: How does Huntress primarily differ from a traditional anti-virus product?

choices.label: Huntress runs on more operating systems.
choices.rationale: OS coverage isn't the primary difference.

choices: Huntress collects telemetry, the SOC analyses and recommends, and the tech actions — while AV blocks autonomously based on signatures.

choices.label: Huntress quarantines files faster.
choices.rationale: Huntress mostly does not autonomously quarantine.

choices.label: Huntress provides a friendlier dashboard for AV alerts.
choices.rationale: Reduces Huntress to a UI on AV; it isn't.

questions.explanation: Notify vs. block, SOC-analyst writeup vs. signatures.

questions.prompt: By the time an Incident Report reaches you, three things have happened. Which?
choices: Triage, classification, and recommendation.

choices.label: Remediation, customer comms, and ticket closure.
choices.rationale: Downstream of the tech's work, not pre-handoff.

choices.label: AV scan, quarantine, and isolation.
Patching, scanning, and reporting.

questions.explanation: The report is the output of analysis, not its input.

questions.prompt: A peer closes an EDR Incident Report as benign because the activity "looks legitimate" to them, with no new signal. What class of mistake is this?

choices.label: Standard triage.
choices.rationale: Overriding the SOC's disposition isn't standard triage.

choices: Second-guessing the SOC — unilateral disagreement without new signal.

choices.label: Customer comms — informing the customer the SOC was wrong.
choices.rationale: Comms isn't the action being described.

choices.label: Following the keystone rule.
choices.rationale: Inverted; the keystone says you don't do this.

questions.explanation: The cardinal mistake.

questions.prompt: Which of these is "new signal" — a legitimate reason to challenge a closed Incident Report?

choices.label: Your gut feels wrong.

choices: A user calling to say they didn't do the thing the report attributes to them.

choices.label: Re-reading the Evidence and reaching the same conclusion.
Reading a blog post about similar-looking activity.
choices.rationale: Not new signal on this specific incident.

questions.explanation: Information the analyst didn't have when they wrote the recommendation.

questions.prompt: A senior who asks "did you action it?" instead of "did you investigate it?" is using vocabulary that maps to the SOC model.
questions.explanation: Acting on the recommendation is the work; investigating from the tech's seat is duplicating the analyst's with worse tooling.

questions.prompt: Managed ITDR covers which identity systems today?

choices.label: Microsoft 365 only.
choices.rationale: True pre-rename; ITDR broadened to also cover Google Workspace.

choices: Microsoft 365 and Google Workspace.

choices.label: Microsoft 365, Google Workspace, and Okta.
On-prem AD and Azure AD.
choices.rationale: ITDR covers M365 and GWS at the SaaS-identity layer.

questions.explanation: ITDR is the rename of MDR for Microsoft 365, broadened to GWS.

questions.prompt: Ransomware canaries are best described as:
choices: A tripwire under Managed EDR; canary trips are Critical by default.

choices.label: A standalone product sold separately from EDR.
An ITDR feature for identity-based ransomware.
A retired product replaced by SIEM.

questions.explanation: Tripwire mechanism delivered alongside EDR, Critical by default on trip.

questions.prompt: Three incidents: (1) Suspicious sign-in, alice@example.com, impossible travel. (2) Process detection, SRV-FILE01, powershell.exe via scheduled task. (3) Canary tripped, WS-FINANCE-04. Which surface produced each?

choices.label: All three are EDR.

choices: 1 is ITDR, 2 is EDR, 3 is a canary trip (under EDR, Critical by default).

choices.label: 1 is ITDR, 2 is SIEM, 3 is EDR.
choices.rationale: Mistakes process detection for a SIEM log event.

choices.label: 1 is SIEM, 2 is EDR, 3 is ITDR.

questions.explanation: UPN vs. hostname is the easiest tell.

questions.prompt: Where should you start your shift in the portal?

choices.label: Organisations — click through each customer.
choices.rationale: Most orgs haven't moved; you'd waste an hour.

choices: Incidents — filtered to open and Critical-first.

choices.label: Reports — pull last week's summary.
choices.rationale: Reports aggregate for monthly client comms.

choices.label: Settings — confirm nothing changed.

questions.explanation: Sort by attention required, not alphabetically.

questions.prompt: A peer reports the Incidents page is "full of old closed Criticals." What's the likely issue?
choices: Filtered by severity but forgot to filter by status; Critical without status: open pulls in months of closed Criticals.

choices.label: Permissions are wrong.
The portal is broken.
Browser cache is stale.

questions.explanation: Severity AND status together.

questions.prompt: The most expensive mistake a new tech makes on this platform is:
choices: Doing the right thing on the wrong customer.

choices.label: Forgetting to document the response.
choices.rationale: Recoverable; not the most expensive class.

choices.label: Closing a ticket too quickly.
Failing to escalate a Low severity.

questions.explanation: Correct action, wrong organisation — a working endpoint offline.

questions.prompt: The lesson recommends a habit comparable to "the driver's glance at the speedometer." What is it?
choices: Reading the org name on the header strip before every destructive action.

choices.label: Refreshing the page every few minutes.
Saying the customer's name out loud before each click.
Re-logging into the portal between customers.

questions.explanation: Cost of the glance is zero; cost of the wrong tenant is hours.

questions.prompt: A senior says: "Isolate the laptop that just popped on Acme — Critical canary trip." You have Acme Manufacturing and Acme Holdings. The portal header shows Acme Holdings. You start to click Isolate. What's the right move?
choices: Don't click yet — pause, open the Critical canary incident to read the affected entity, confirm which Acme it's in, then act with the header confirmed.

choices.label: Click Isolate immediately — Critical means contain first.
choices.rationale: Contain-first doesn't license action on the wrong customer.

choices.label: Isolate the laptop in both Acmes to be safe.
Ask the senior to repeat themselves louder.
choices.rationale: The incident itself names the affected entity.

questions.explanation: Critical urgency doesn't license a wrong-tenant click. The incident has the affected entity.

questions.prompt: You filter Incidents to "open, Critical-first" and find three Criticals all tagged to the same organisation. What does the pattern tell you?
choices: One customer is having a bad day. Open the first incident and tell the senior in parallel — three Criticals in one org is worth flagging.

choices.label: The Incidents view is misconfigured; refresh the filters.
Bundle the three into one investigation.
choices.rationale: Critical containment is one-at-a-time per the playbook.

choices.label: Wait for the senior to contact you.
choices.rationale: Reactive; the lesson teaches proactive escalation.

questions.explanation: Three Criticals on one tenant is tell-the-senior-in-parallel territory.

questions.prompt: The response shape for a Critical incident differs from Low and High. How?
choices: Contain first (isolate or run the compromise playbook), escalate in parallel, do not investigate.

choices.label: Same shape as High but with a 1-hour SLA.
Investigate first, then contain.
Wait for a senior to take over completely.

questions.explanation: Forensics is above the helpdesk ceiling.

questions.prompt: Who sets the severity band on an Incident Report?
choices: The SOC analyst. The tech doesn't re-grade unilaterally; they can ask the analyst to revisit.

choices.label: The customer's IT contact based on impact.
The on-call senior.
An automated rule the tech can configure.

questions.explanation: Severity is the SOC's call.

questions.prompt: You read an incident: Low severity, but the Recommendation says "isolate the host immediately." What's the right move?

choices.label: Ignore the recommendation; if it's Low, you don't isolate.
choices.rationale: Ignoring the Recommendation is the cardinal mistake.

choices: Send a clarifying reply to the analyst — the tag and text don't match, and it's worth confirming.

choices.label: Isolate immediately and ignore the mismatch.
choices.rationale: A 30-second reply costs nothing before something irreversible.

choices.label: Re-grade to Critical to match the Recommendation.

questions.explanation: Mismatch is information; the right surface is a clarifying reply.

questions.prompt: Order the steps of reading an Incident Report.
items: Header — severity, surface, affected entity, customer
Recommendation — what does the SOC want done?
Summary if the Recommendation surprised you
Evidence and Timeline as you need them — client comms, replies, documentation
questions.explanation: Top-down ends in the cardinal mistake. Recommendation-first installs the right reflex.

questions.prompt: Which Incident Report section is the one you ACT ON?

choices.label: Evidence.

choices: Recommendation.

choices.label: Summary.
choices.rationale: Context. Read on a need-to basis.

choices.label: Header.

questions.explanation: The Recommendation is the action zone — the section you act on, full stop.

questions.prompt: The Timeline view is appropriate for which of the following uses?
choices: Walking a client through the sequence of events, and verifying containment caught the activity in time.

choices.label: Building a case to override the Recommendation.
Replacing the Recommendation when you have time pressure.
Generating an automated summary for the PSA.

questions.explanation: Client comms and containment verification, not as a counter-argument to the Recommendation.

questions.prompt: The cardinal mistake in reading an Incident Report is:
choices: Acting on the context zone (Summary, Evidence, Timeline) instead of (or in addition to) the action zone (Recommendation).

choices.label: Replying to the analyst with a clarifying question.
Reading the Recommendation first.
choices.rationale: Inverted — that's the correct reading order.

choices.label: Verifying the affected host before acting.
choices.rationale: That's the wrong-host check, also correct.

questions.explanation: Evidence treated as a to-do list is the named cardinal mistake.

questions.prompt: What yes/no test does the lesson teach you to run before any action?
choices: Did the analyst say to do this, in the Recommendation? If yes, proceed. If no, stop.

choices.label: Is severity Critical? If yes, contain. If no, investigate.
choices.rationale: Severity drives tempo, not the action-vs-context test.

choices.label: Is the customer aware?
Has Huntress support approved?

questions.explanation: "No, but the Evidence supports it" is the cardinal-mistake signature.

questions.prompt: A High EDR incident lands. Recommendation: approve the autoruns remediation. The customer's IT contact phones in saying he set up the autoruns entry yesterday for a legitimate purpose. What do you do?

choices.label: Reduce the severity to Low yourself and close the incident.
choices.rationale: Two mistakes — re-grading and unilateral closure.

choices: Reply to the analyst with the customer context (new signal) and wait for disposition; don't approve and don't close.

choices.label: Approve the remediation anyway; the SOC's call stands.
choices.rationale: Acts before the layer-that-decides has weighed the new context.

choices.label: Escalate to a senior because the customer disagrees with the SOC.
choices.rationale: The analyst is the right body, not the senior.

questions.explanation: New-signal channel + don't re-grade. Two lessons combined.

questions.prompt: A Low EDR incident lands. Recommendation: no action required, closing as benign. Evidence shows the process did several things that look unusual to you. What do you do?

choices.label: Reopen the incident to take another look — the Evidence supports it.
choices.rationale: Reopen without new signal is second-guessing in slow motion; acting on Evidence to override "no action" is the cardinal mistake.

choices: Accept the disposition, document, move on. If something specific in the Evidence concerns you, ask the analyst a factual question — but unease alone isn't new signal.

choices.label: Run the remediation anyway, just to be safe.
choices.rationale: Running remediation on a closed-as-benign incident is the cardinal mistake at its most expensive.

choices.label: Re-grade to High because the Evidence seemed serious.

questions.explanation: New-signal rule + action-vs-context test combined.

questions.prompt: You're triaging a queue and need to route incidents to the right playbook. The course gives a one-step heuristic before you read the body. What is it?
choices: Identify the surface from the Header (EDR, ITDR, SIEM, canary). The surface tells you which playbook applies before you read a word of the body.

choices.label: Identify the severity first. The band tells you which playbook applies.
Read the Summary first. The Summary tells you the playbook.
Read every section in order to be thorough.
choices.rationale: Wrong reading order, and "thorough" on this platform isn't reading everything.

questions.explanation: Surface drives playbook; severity drives tempo.
